title: Foreign aid given for emergencies and longer-term development
subtitle: Committed bilateral [official development assistance (ODA)](#dod:oda) from governments and multilateral organizations, grants from civil society organizations. Aid for emergencies, such as natural disasters and wars, is often called humanitarian aid. This data is expressed in US dollars and adjusted for inflation.
caption:
Foreign aid given for emergencies and longer-term development, DAC countries, 1971 to 2024

Committed bilateral official development assistance (ODA) from governments and multilateral organizations, grants from civil society organizations. Aid for emergencies, such as natural disasters and wars, is often called humanitarian aid. This data is expressed in US dollars and adjusted for inflation.

The stacked area chart displays foreign aid flows from 1971 to 2024, measured in constant 2023 US dollars. The visualization is divided into two distinct categories: a lower pink band representing aid for emergencies and a larger upper green band representing aid for longer-term development. Throughout the entire period, the green area constitutes the majority of the total volume. In 1971, the combined total sits between $40 billion and $60 billion. The aggregate value rises gradually through the 1970s and 1980s, reaching a local peak near $100 billion around 1991 before dipping back toward $60 billion in the late 1990s. From 2000 onward, the total volume exhibits a sustained upward trajectory, crossing the $100 billion mark in the mid-2000s and surpassing $140 billion around 2015. The series reaches its highest point in 2023, where the total height exceeds $180 billion, followed by a decline in 2024 to a level between $160 billion and $180 billion. The pink band for emergency aid remains relatively thin for most of the timeline, generally staying below $20 billion, though it shows visible expansion in the final decade, rising to occupy the space between $20 billion and $40 billion by 2024.
